title: Creating a Parallax Scroll Image Slider with Framer Motion and Tailwind CSS
author: Gaurav Kumar
description: In this blog post, I'll show you how I built a smooth parallax scroll image slider using Framer Motion, Next.js, and Tailwind CSS. This component creates a beautiful horizontal scroll effect that reacts to vertical scrolling — perfect for galleries, hero sections, or adding subtle depth to any landing page.

seo.title: Parallax Scroll Image Slider – Framer Motion & Tailwind CSS
seo.description: Learn how to build a smooth parallax scroll image slider in React using Framer Motion and Tailwind CSS. Add depth and interactivity to your Next.js projects with ease.

props.heading: Understanding the Component
props.explanation: The component displays two rows of images that move horizontally in opposite directions as the user scrolls vertically. This creates a smooth, layered parallax effect that brings depth to your UI — perfect for galleries, hero sections, or showcasing portfolios.

props.heading: Setting Up the Environment
props.explanation: We are using React, Framer Motion and Tailwind CSS for styling.

props.heading: Setting Up the Image Rows
props.explanation: Before we add any motion or scrolling effects, let’s start with the basic layout — two horizontal rows of images stacked vertically:

props.heading: Adding Scroll-Based Parallax Motion
props.explanation: With the static image rows set up, it’s time to bring them to life with a smooth parallax effect. In this step, you’ll use Framer Motion’s useScroll and useTransform hooks.

props.heading: 🔍 What does this do?
list: Track how much you’ve scrolled through the image container.
Map that scroll progress to horizontal movement for both rows.
Wrap the rows in <motion.div> so they animate as you scroll.

props.heading: Attach a ref
props.explanation: Attach a ref to the container. This tells useScroll exactly which section to watch:

props.heading: Track scroll progress
list: scrollYProgress goes from 0 → 1 as the container enters and leaves the viewport.
The offset controls when the animation starts and ends.

props.heading: Map scroll progress to horizontal motion
list: The left row moves left up to -150px.
The right row moves right up to +100px.

props.code:
const xLeft = useTransform(scrollYProgress, [0, 1], [0, -150]);
const xRight = useTransform(scrollYProgress, [0, 1], [0, 100]);

props.explanation: Wrap rows in <motion.div> and bind the x transform using the style prop — this makes the rows slide smoothly as you scroll.

props.heading: 🎉 Conclusion
props.explanation: You've successfully created a smooth Parallax Scroll Image Slider component! This demonstrates how simple scroll-based animations can add depth and interactivity to your Next.js projects with Framer Motion and Tailwind CSS.
